title: Spokeshave shaping
subtitle: Using the flat-sole spokeshave to refine curved surfaces on green and seasoned timber
excerpt: The spokeshave is a two-handled small plane that shaves convex and concave curves clean after the drawknife or axe has done the bulk removal. On a stool leg, a chair rung, or a tool handle, the spokeshave brings the surface to a consistent taper and removes the marks left by heavier tools. This technique covers the grip, the blade-adjustment drill, and the grain-direction rules that prevent the spokeshave from digging in.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the spokeshave geometry and adjustment method. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for the spokeshave in curved-surface finish work.

content.text: The
spokeshave

sits between the drawknife and the sandpaper in the workflow. The drawknife brings a stool leg or chair rung close to its final size. The spokeshave then smooths the surface to a steady taper without taking off much wood. In the right hands a well-tuned spokeshave is the fastest way to turn a rough-axed or drawknife-shaped blank into a clean round surface.
What it does

The spokeshave's blade is set at a low angle in a mouth; the two handles extend from each side. The maker grips both handles and pushes or pulls the tool across the workpiece, guiding the blade by wrist and arm movement. The depth of cut is set by adjusting the blade depth; a shallow set produces a fine shaving finish, a deep set removes material quickly. The flat-sole version rides on convex surfaces; the round-sole version dips into concave faces.
When to use it

After the drawknife or axe on green-wood legs, rungs, handles, and any cylindrical or tapered piece. On seasoned timber for the same finishing purpose: a spoke, a hammer handle, a mallet shaft. The spokeshave cannot take as much material as the drawknife in a pass; use the heavier tools first.
What you need
A flat-sole spokeshave with a sharp blade. An India oilstone for honing the blade; a leather strop for maintenance during use. The workpiece clamped in a
shaving horse
or held in a vice. The spokeshave requires both hands, so the work must be secured independently.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Set the blade depth

The blade should protrude about 0.3 mm below the sole. Too deep and the tool digs in and chatters; too shallow and it does not cut. The test: make a single pass on scrap wood. The shaving should be thin and translucent, falling cleanly away. If nothing comes off, advance the blade a quarter-turn; if the tool chatters and digs, retract a quarter-turn.
Step 2: Find the grain direction

The spokeshave cuts with the grain exactly like any other cutting tool. On a tapered leg, the grain typically runs from the thick end toward the thin end; push the spokeshave from thick toward thin. If the tool chatters or the surface tears, the grain has reversed; pull from the thin end toward the thick instead, or work from the centre outward toward both ends.
Step 3: The grip and stance

Grip the spokeshave handles with both thumbs behind the tool body and the fingers wrapped around the front of the handles. This grip gives positive control over the depth of cut by wrist rotation: tilting the handles toward the workpiece deepens the cut, tilting away shallows it. The arms do the pushing; the wrists do the steering.
Step 4: Work in overlapping passes

Make passes along the full length of the workpiece, overlapping each pass by half the blade width. Rotate the workpiece between passes so the spokeshave addresses every face. On a cylinder, four overlapping passes at ninety-degree rotations produces an even surface.
What it looks like when it is right

Thin, even, slightly translucent shavings falling cleanly from the mouth. The cut surface is smooth to the touch with no ridges or tool marks remaining from the drawknife. On green wood, the surface may look slightly glossy from the moisture.
Common mistakes

Blade set too deep: the tool chatters, catches, and digs. Retract the blade and return to the shallow setting. On green wood, the extra moisture resistance means a shallower setting is needed than on the same species seasoned.

Blade not square in the mouth: the spokeshave cuts deeper on one side than the other. Check the blade angle with a straightedge across the sole; adjust the blade-cap screws to equalise the depth across the full width.
Cutting against the grain: the surface tears and the tool resists rather than cutting. Reverse direction; on a complex taper the grain may reverse midway along the piece.
